title: Split AC BTU Calculator by Room Size 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: How Much BTU for Split AC by Room Size?
description: Calculate exact BTU needed for split AC units based on room size and climate. Free, fast, and accurate cooling sizing.
intro: This calculator determines the correct BTU (British Thermal Unit) capacity needed for a split air conditioner based on your room's square footage and local climate. BTU is the standard unit measuring how much heat an AC unit can remove per hour. The core formula is: **BTU = Room Area (m²) × Climate Factor (BTU/m²)**. Climate factors range from 600 BTU/m² in mild zones to 900+ BTU/m² in hot/humid regions. Getting this right is critical: an undersized unit runs non-stop and never cools properly, while an oversized unit short-cycles, leaving the air humid and uncomfortable. Use this tool before buying any split AC unit to match capacity to your space.
keyTakeaway: **BTU = m² × Climate Factor** — e.g., 20 m² in a warm climate: 20 × 700 = 14,000 BTU → choose a 12,000–14,000 BTU split (nominal "2500F" or "18,000 BTU" next step up). 1 BTU/h ≈ 0.252 kcal/h (frigorías).
useCases: Choosing the right split AC unit before purchase for a 15 m² bedroom in a hot southern state like Texas or Florida
Sizing a mini-split for a converted garage of 40 m² with poor insulation and direct sun exposure
Replacing an old window unit with a ductless split in a 25 m² living room to reduce electricity bills
Calculating cooling needs for a home office in a temperate climate (e.g., Pacific Northwest) where humidity is low but summer peaks occur
Verifying that a landlord-installed 9,000 BTU split is actually sufficient for a 22 m² apartment room in a warm climate
seoKeywords: how many btu do i need
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The fundamental formula used by HVAC engineers and endorsed by ENERGY STAR guidelines is:

```
BTU/h = Room Area (m²) × Climate Factor (BTU/m²)

Frigorías (kcal/h) = BTU/h × 0.252

Example:
  Room = 20 m², Warm climate (Factor = 700)
  BTU/h = 20 × 700 = 14,000 BTU/h
  Frigorías = 14,000 × 0.252 = 3,528 kcal/h
  → Select: 12,000–14,000 BTU split (nominal "1 ton" to "1.25 ton")
```

The climate factor captures the combined effect of outdoor temperature, solar gain, humidity, and typical insulation levels for each zone. The DOE's Manual J methodology uses similar load calculations; this formula is a practical approximation widely used for residential split AC sizing.

---

## Reference Table

### Climate Factor by Zone

| Climate Type | Typical US Regions | BTU per m² |
|---|---|---|
| Mild / Temperate | Pacific Northwest, Northern CA | 500–600 |
| Moderate | Mid-Atlantic, Midwest, Pacific Coast | 600–700 |
| Warm | Southeast, Southwest, Central TX | 700–800 |
| Hot & Humid | South FL, Gulf Coast, Puerto Rico | 800–900 |
| Hot & Dry | Phoenix AZ, Las Vegas NV | 850–950 |

### BTU to Commercial Split AC Size

| BTU Range | Frigorías (kcal/h) | Commercial Label | Nominal Tons |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7,000–9,000 | 1,764–2,268 | 9,000 BTU / "0750F" | 0.75 ton |
| 9,001–12,000 | 2,268–3,024 | 12,000 BTU / "1000F" | 1 ton |
| 12,001–14,000 | 3,024–3,528 | 14,000 BTU / "1200F" | 1 ton+ |
| 14,001–18,000 | 3,528–4,536 | 18,000 BTU / "1500F" | 1.5 ton |
| 18,001–24,000 | 4,536–6,048 | 24,000 BTU / "2000F" | 2 ton |
| 24,001–30,000 | 6,048–7,560 | 30,000 BTU / "2500F" | 2.5 ton |
| 30,001–36,000 | 7,560–9,072 | 36,000 BTU / "3000F" | 3 ton |

> **Note:** Always round up to the next available commercial size — never down.

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Bedroom, 15 m², Moderate Climate (Mid-Atlantic)
```
BTU = 15 × 650 = 9,750 BTU/h
Frigorías = 9,750 × 0.252 = 2,457 kcal/h
→ Choose: 12,000 BTU split (next size up from 9,750)
```
A 9,000 BTU unit would be marginal on hot days; the 12,000 BTU model with an inverter compressor is the standard recommendation and will modulate down to avoid over-cooling.

### Case 2 — Living/Dining Room, 35 m², Hot & Humid (Gulf Coast)
```
BTU = 35 × 850 = 29,750 BTU/h
Frigorías = 29,750 × 0.252 = 7,497 kcal/h
→ Choose: 30,000 BTU split (2.5 ton)
```
In high-humidity zones, latent heat load (moisture removal) is significant. If the room has large south-facing windows, add 10% to the base BTU result.

### Case 3 — Home Office, 20 m², Mild Climate (Seattle, WA)
```
BTU = 20 × 550 = 11,000 BTU/h
Frigorías = 11,000 × 0.252 = 2,772 kcal/h
→ Choose: 12,000 BTU split
```
A home office with 2 occupants and active computers adds ~1,000 BTU of internal load. The 12,000 BTU inverter unit comfortably covers this with efficiency headroom.

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using square footage (ft²) instead of square meters (m²) in the formula.** 1 m² = 10.764 ft². Plugging ft² values directly will oversize your unit by ~10×. Always convert first or use the ft²-based factor (~65–90 BTU/ft²).

2. **Ignoring ceiling height.** The base formula assumes a standard 2.5 m (8 ft) ceiling. Rooms taller than 3 m (10 ft) need a volume correction: multiply BTU result by (actual ceiling height ÷ 2.5).

3. **Not accounting for sun exposure.** South- and west-facing rooms with large glass windows can receive 400–600 W/m² of solar gain. Add 10–15% to the BTU result for heavy sun exposure rooms.

4. **Choosing the smallest unit to save money.** An undersized split runs at 100% capacity continuously, wears out faster, and never reaches the setpoint temperature. ENERGY STAR data shows it can use 20–30% more electricity than a correctly sized inverter unit.

5. **Ignoring internal heat sources.** Each person in a room adds ~250–400 BTU/h. A kitchen adds 4,000+ BTU/h from cooking. Offices with multiple computers and monitors can add 1,000–3,000 BTU/h of load.

6. **Assuming all "18,000 BTU" units are equal.** EER (Energy Efficiency Ratio) and SEER (Seasonal EER) ratings vary widely. The DOE mandates minimum SEER2 of 13.4 for split systems ≤45,000 BTU in the US (effective 2023). Higher SEER2 = lower electricity bills.

---

## Related Calculators

- [Energy cost calculator](/calculadora-costo-energia)
- [Room volume calculator](/calculadora-volumen-habitacion)
- [Electricity consumption calculator](/calculadora-consumo-electrico)

faq.q: How many BTU do I need for a 200 sq ft (≈18.6 m²) room?
faq.a: In a moderate climate: 18.6 m² × 650 BTU/m² ≈ 12,090 BTU/h. The ENERGY STAR sizing guide recommends approximately 6,000–14,000 BTU for rooms in the 100–300 sq ft range depending on insulation and climate. For a standard 200 sq ft room, a 12,000 BTU (1-ton) split AC is the standard recommendation.

faq.q: What is the difference between BTU and frigorías (kcal/h)?
faq.a: Both measure cooling (or heating) power, just in different unit systems. 1 BTU/h = 0.252 kcal/h, so 1 frigoría = 1 kcal/h ≈ 3.968 BTU/h. In Spanish-speaking and European markets, split AC units are often rated in frigorías. A 12,000 BTU unit equals roughly 3,024 kcal/h (3,024 frigorías).

faq.q: Does climate really change how many BTU I need?
faq.a: Yes, significantly. The DOE's load calculation methodology (ASHRAE Manual J) factors in outdoor design temperature, humidity, and solar radiation. A room in Miami (design temp ~93°F/34°C, high humidity) may need 30–50% more BTU than the same room in San Francisco (design temp ~73°F/23°C, low humidity). Using the wrong climate factor is one of the most common sizing errors.

faq.q: Should I choose a fixed-speed or inverter split AC?
faq.a: For most residential applications, an inverter (variable-speed) compressor is strongly preferred. Inverter units modulate output between ~30–100% of rated capacity, saving 25–40% in electricity vs. fixed-speed units per ENERGY STAR data. They also maintain more stable temperatures and have longer compressor life. The upfront cost premium is typically recovered in 2–3 years of electricity savings.

faq.q: What is SEER2 and why does it matter for my split AC?
faq.a: SEER2 (Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio 2) is the updated DOE efficiency metric effective January 2023. It measures total cooling output (BTU) divided by total electrical energy used (Wh) over a cooling season under revised test conditions. The federal minimum for split systems ≤45,000 BTU is SEER2 13.4 in most US regions (14.3 in the Southeast and Southwest). Higher SEER2 = lower electricity bills; top units reach SEER2 20–26.

faq.q: How do I convert BTU to tons of refrigeration?
faq.a: 1 ton of refrigeration = 12,000 BTU/h, a unit derived from the heat absorbed by melting one short ton (2,000 lb) of ice in 24 hours. So a 24,000 BTU split = 2 tons; an 18,000 BTU split = 1.5 tons. This ton-based naming is standard in the US HVAC industry and on most equipment spec sheets.

faq.q: Does the BTU formula change if the room is a kitchen?
faq.a: Yes. Kitchens have major internal heat loads from cooking appliances. The ASHRAE handbook recommends adding 4,000–6,000 BTU/h for a residential kitchen with a gas or electric range. So a 15 m² kitchen in a warm climate would need: (15 × 750) + 5,000 = 11,250 + 5,000 = 16,250 BTU/h → select an 18,000 BTU unit, not the 12,000 BTU you'd use for a bedroom of the same size.

faq.q: Can I use one large split AC to cool multiple rooms?
faq.a: A single-zone split AC cools only the room where the indoor unit is installed; adjacent rooms rely on air circulation through open doors, which is inefficient and uneven. For multi-room cooling, a multi-zone (multi-split) system connects 2–5 indoor units to one outdoor compressor. Each indoor zone should be sized independently using the BTU formula for that room's area and climate load.

faq.q: What happens if I install a split AC that is too large (oversized)?
faq.a: An oversized unit 'short-cycles' — it reaches the thermostat setpoint very quickly and shuts off before removing enough moisture from the air. This leaves the room feeling cold but clammy. The frequent on-off cycles also stress the compressor, reducing lifespan. The EPA/ENERGY STAR program specifically warns against oversizing; the goal is to size within 15% of the calculated load, not simply 'bigger is better.'

sources.name: ENERGY STAR — Room Air Conditioner Key Product Criteria
US DOE — Federal Energy Conservation Standards for Residential Central Air Conditioners (SEER2)
ASHRAE — Handbook of Fundamentals (Manual J Load Calculations overview)

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
